coredata.dc:title: Excellent behaviors of highly dispersed Ni-based catalyst in CO methanation synthesized by in-situ hydrothermal method with carbon quantum dots assisted
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Wenli
Yin, Qiangfeng
Meng, Xin
He, Xuelian
Xin, Zhong
coredata.dc:description: Confining the active metal particles inside the channel of mesoporous support is an effective way to improve the catalytic activity and stability, as well as enhance the metal usage thus reducing the required amount of supported metal. In our research, a novel highly efficient Nickel-based catalyst with the metal particles confined in the pores of mesoporous support was obtained by in-situ hydrothermal synthesis with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) assisted. This catalyst is of scientific significance for low metal content (as low as 0.9 wt%), except for its large specific surface area, high metal dispersion and small metal particle size. And the catalyst exhibits satisfying CO methanation catalytic activity with 95% CO conversion and 90% CH4 selectivity at 400 °C and 15000 mlg−1h−1 when the metal loading is 0.9 wt%. Meanwhile, the catalyst presents excellent thermal and long-term stability for that the metal particles are confined inside the pores of support, which could strengthen the metal-support interaction and thus inhibiting the metal agglomeration and carbon deposition effectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Nickel-based
Supported catalyst
High dispersion
CO methanation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of CQDs and catalyst precursor solution
Properties of catalysts based on CQDs
The key role of CQDs in preparation of catalysts

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC No. 21808062 , 21808064 , and 21776091 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (a) and corresponding pore width distribution (b) of the calcined catalysts.
XRD patterns of calcined (a) and reduced (b) catalysts.
Deconvolution XPS spectra of Ni 2p (a) and XPS spectrum of O 1s (b) on the reduced catalysts.
Catalytic performance of the catalysts. (a) CO conversion; (b) CH4 selectivity; (c) CO2 and C2-C4 selectivity.
Energy diagram for CO dissociation and carbon species hydrogenation on Ni (111) facet.
Long-term test of catalysts. (a) CO conversion; (b) CH4 selectivity.
XRD patterns of catalysts after thermal stability.
TEM images of catalysts after achieving thermal stability. The inserted image is different areas of the same catalyst. (a) 5Ni/SiO2; (b) 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs.

(a) UV/Vis absorption spectra of suspensions in aqueous solutions, (b) full-wavelength PL spectrum of CQDs solutions, (c) PL spectrum of catalyst precursor solutions, (d) FT-IR spectrum of dried CQDs and that dissolved Ni(NO3)2. D1, CQDs; D2, Ni-SiO2-CQDs; D3, Ni-SiO2; D4, H2O.
The synthetic strategy for highly dispersed Ni-based catalyst with metal particles confined in the pores of support.
Physical and chemical properties of the catalysts.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Specific surface area calculated by the BET equation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Pore volume obtained according to BJH desorption cumulative pore volume.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Metal reduction degree at reduction temperature.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: f
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Metal dispersion and metallic surface measured based on H2-pulse chemisorption results.

head.ce:title: Excellent behaviors of highly dispersed Ni-based catalyst in CO methanation synthesized by in-situ hydrothermal method with carbon quantum dots assisted

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Multiphase Materials Chemical Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, 130 Meilong Road, Shanghai 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Multiphase Materials Chemical Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Multiphase Materials Chemical Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, 130 Meilong Road, Shanghai 200237, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, 130 Meilong Road, Shanghai, 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, 130 Meilong Road, Shanghai, 200237, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: East China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Confining the active metal particles inside the channel of mesoporous support is an effective way to improve the catalytic activity and stability, as well as enhance the metal usage thus reducing the required amount of supported metal. In our research, a novel highly efficient Nickel-based catalyst with the metal particles confined in the pores of mesoporous support was obtained by in-situ hydrothermal synthesis with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) assisted. This catalyst is of scientific significance for low metal content (as low as 0.9 wt%), except for its large specific surface area, high metal dispersion and small metal particle size. And the catalyst exhibits satisfying CO methanation catalytic activity with 95% CO conversion and 90% CH4 selectivity at 400 °C and 15000 mlg−1h−1 when the metal loading is 0.9 wt%. Meanwhile, the catalyst presents excellent thermal and long-term stability for that the metal particles are confined inside the pores of support, which could strengthen the metal-support interaction and thus inhibiting the metal agglomeration and carbon deposition effectively.

ce:section.ce:para: The low efficiency and environmental pollution of direct coal combustion have been major public concerns for the past decades, while synthetic natural gas (SNG) has been continuously gaining popularity as a clean and efficient energy source [1,2]. The efficient catalytic conversion of syngas (CO + H2) from coal to methane has been regarded as one of the promising strategies to enhance the energy utilization efficiency, thus the design of methanation catalyst becomes the committed step.

Among transition metals in the group VIII element, Ni-based catalyst has been widely studied in methanation for its comparable activity in CO dissociation and hydrogenation [3] with noble metal catalyst. Researches have shown that metal with nanoscale can significantly improve the catalytic efficiency, reduce the amount of active metal, and enhance the metal utilization [4–6]. The catalytic activity and selectivity per surface metal atom depend strongly on the particle size [7,8], and for Ni based methanation catalyst, as the nanoparticle size decreases, the CO dissociation and intermediate hydrogenation accelerates [9].

However, Ni metal particles are prone to migrate and agglomerate to large particles via Ostwald ripening at a higher temperature due to its low Tammann temperature [10,11]. Therefore, γ-Al2O3 [11–13] and mesoporous molecular sieves with large specific surface area [14–18] as support is regarded as the effective way to improve the metal dispersion to nanometer size to prevent metal agglomeration. Studies have shown that Ni particles are dispersed outside the pores of molecular sieve mostly, and due to the weak interaction between the metal particles and the support, metal particles easily agglomerate into large ones, thus leading to the catalyst deactivation. Furthermore, with the decrease of nanoparticle size, the surface binding energy increase seriously, by which metal particles are inclined to agglomerate into big particles [19,20]. Thus, it is not only necessary to adjust the metal particles in nanosize, but also to confine the metal nanoparticles into the channel of mesoporous support in order to strengthen the metal-support interaction and prevent the metal particles agglomerate. The addition of small organic molecules as polyol [21,22], alkane [23–25] could increase the surface Si-OH group and enhance the hydrophilicity of support, then promote the metal particles dispersion in the pore of support, leading to a stronger metal-support interaction, which would prevent the sintering of metal particles [17,26,27]. Nevertheless, only a small part of metal particles was introduced into the pores of support by co-impregnation method.

In order to enhance the proportion of particles in the pores of support, we adopted an in-situ hydrothermal method to complex the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with metal ions and then dispersed inside the support to obtain a novel highly efficient Ni-based CO methanation catalyst. There are abundant functional groups on the flexible surface of CQDs [28,29], such as –COOH and –NH2, and these groups could coordinate with most metals with empty d orbits to form a relatively stable CQDs-metal ions composite material with a certain size, which make CQDs widely applied in the detection of ultra-low concentration metal ions, photocatalysis and electrochemical catalysis [30–32]. Inspired by the complexation effect of CQDs with metal ions, we confined the metal particles into the pores of support by in-situ hydrothermal synthesis with CQDs assisted, and catalysts with different metal loading (1-5 wt%) were obtained. Catalyst precursor solutions were characterized by UV–Vis spectrophotometer, Dark box three-use UV analyzer, full wavelength PL spectrum and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) to determine the formation of CQDs and the complexation between CQDs and metal ions. The correlation between catalyst performance and physiochemical properties of the synthesized catalyst was studied by Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), N2-physical adsorption and desorption, X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), H2-pulse chemisorption, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), H2-temperature programmed reduction (H2-TPR). The active energy of the CO dissociation and CHx hydrogenation on the (111) plane of Ni was calculated by density functional theory (DFT).

Catalysts were prepared according to the Patent CN202010155699:A [33]. 10.8 g Citric acid (CA, AR) and 1.6 g ethanediamine (EDA, AR) were dissolved into 125 g deionized water. Then 14.5 g tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, AR) was dropped into the solution. Finally, 0.31 g, 0.62 g, and 1.53 g nickle nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, AR) were added into the mixtures. After stirring continuously for 8 h, the mixture solution was transferred to a teflon and kept 12 h at 140 °C in a homogeneous reactor. The suspension was filtered, dried and then calcined at 550 °C for 5 h in air atmosphere. The solid was grinded and catalysts with Ni loading of 1 wt%, 2 wt% and 5 wt% were obtained, written as 1Ni-SiO2-CQDs, 2Ni-SiO2-CQDs, 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs. For comparison, two catalysts, 5Ni/SiO2 and 5Ni-SiO2 were prepared. For 5Ni/SiO2, crystalline SiO2 of 200 mesh quartz was used as support, traditional impregnation method was selected and Ni loading was theoretically 5 wt%, then the dry and calcination process are the same with xNi-SiO2-CQDs catalyst. And for 5Ni-SiO2 catalyst, the preparation process was similar with the 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs, and the adding amount of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O was the same as 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs, while there were no CA and EDA in the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and catalyst precursor solution
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis spectrophotometer and Dark box three-use UV analyzer were used to confirm the formation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) during the preparation process. Full wavelength photoluminescence spectrum (PL spectrum) was to ensure the formation of CQDs and judge the interaction between CQDs and metal ion. The surface functional groups of CQDs and CQDs-Ni2+ were detected through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR).

Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, ThermoScientific icapQ) was used to detect the metal content in catalysts. Micromeritics ASAP 2020 was to ensure the specific surface area, pore volume and pore size distribution from N2 adsorption/desorption at liquid nitrogen temperature (−196 °C). Bruker advanced D8 powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Kα (λ = 1.5418 Å) at 40 kV and 40 mA was selected to characterize the crystallographic structures. JEM-2100 microscope at a 200 kV acceleration voltage was used to obtain the high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of reduced catalyst to observe the metal distribution intuitively. Micromeritics Auto Chem II was used to record the reduction properties (H2 temperature-programmed reduction, H2-TPR), and to measure Ni metal dispersion in catalysts (H2 pulse chemisorption). The metal dispersion of Ni was calculated based on the H2-pulse chemisorption results using the following equation:
D
( % )
=
2 ×
V
ad
× M × S F
m × P ×
V m
×
d r
× 100
where, Vad (mL) is the volume of chemisorbed H2 at the standard conditions (temperature and pressure) measured in the H2-pulse chemisorption experiment; M is the molar mass of Ni (58.69 gmol−1); SF is the stoichiometric factor (the Ni:H ratio in the chemisorption, assuming the adsorption stoichiometry of H/Ni = 1:1); m (g) is the weight of reduced sample; P is the weight fraction of Ni in sample; Vm is the molar volume of gas (22.4 Lmol−1); dr is the reduction degree of Ni calculated by H2-TPR.

ESCALAB 210Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) instrument with a monochromatized Al Kα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV) was used to detect the valance state, binding energy of Ni elements as well as the surface element distribution on catalysts after reduction. Nova NanoSEM 450 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with 3 kV acceleration voltage was used for an intuitional looking of reduced catalysts surface. VARIO EL III CHNS element with a high temperature combustion furnace, adsorption–desorption device and thermal conductivity detector (TCD) was to measure the content of carbon in catalyst before and after thermal stability. DFT theory was used to calculate the energy barrier during the dissociation and hydrogenation on the surface of Ni (111) facet. The unit cell parameters a = b = c = 3.525, Kpoint adopts 1 × 1 × 1 and the optimization and transition state conditions are consistent.

The catalyst activity studies for syngas methanation were conducted in a stainless steel fixed bed reactor with an inner diameter of 8 mm at atmospheric pressure and temperature range of 200–500 °C, and the catalyst bed length is 4 mm. The thermocouple directly located in the middle of the catalyst bed. The reaction temperature was detected by a K-type thermocouple and controlled by an air condensing system. 0.2 g xNi-SiO2-CQDs and 5Ni/SiO2 catalyst were activated in pure H2 with a flow of 20 ml/min at 500 °C for 2 h before activity test, while the 5Ni-SiO2 catalysts was reduced at 640 °C for 2 h. Afterwards, the reactant mixtures, H2, N2, and CO were co-fed into the micro-reactor with a molar ratio of 3/1/1, and the GHSV (gas hourly space velocity) was kept 15000 ml g-1 cath−1. The outlet mixture was cooled down to room temperature to separate water from the gas, then the gas was introduced into an on-line gas chromatography (GC7900, Techcomp). The GC was equipped with a flame ionization detector (FID 1) using a plot-Q capillary column to detect C2-C4 species and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) with a TDX-01 packed column to detect CO, CH4 and CO2 with high concentration; and CO, CH4 and CO2 with low concentration were detected by FID 2 through a reformer. The catalytic activity of catalyst was evaluated by conversion of CO (
X CO
), and selectivity of CH4, CO2, C2-C4 (
S

,
S

,
S

-

) after the reaction temperature reached stable for 1 h, and the calculation methods were as the following equations:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of catalysts based on CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The dispersion and particle size of active metal on support in catalysts can be characterized by XRD, H2-pulse chemisorption and TEM. From the XRD spectra of the catalyst precursor before calcination (Fig. S1), it can be seen that there is a broad peak of amorphous carbon in CQDs, and a broad peak of amorphous silicon in Ni-SiO2-CQDs. In addition to the broad peak of amorphous silicon in Ni-SiO2 precursor, there is a vague peak of nickel silicate hydrate. It is difficult to see obvious difference between catalysts precursor from XRD before calcination. However, it can be seen from the XRD spectrum of the catalyst after calcination (Fig. 2 a) that there are obvious diffraction peaks of NiO in 5Ni/SiO2, while there are no diffraction peaks of NiO in Ni-SiO2-CQDs and Ni-SiO2. The XRD patterns of the catalyst after calcination show that the NiO content in the catalyst prepared by hydrothermal method is low or the particles are highly dispersed. From the XRD patterns of reduced catalysts (Fig. 2b), the 5Ni/SiO2 catalyst prepared via impregnation method displays Ni and SiO2 peaks according to the JCPDS PDF# 04–0850 and PDF# 46–1045. For xNi-SiO2-CQDs and Ni-SiO2 catalysts, the characteristic peaks of Ni and SiO2 disappear, and the wide peak appeared at 23° belongs to amorphous silica, which means that the support is amorphous SiO2. The disappearance of Ni characteristic peaks demonstrates that Ni is highly dispersed on SiO2 or the Ni particles are amorphous [37].

From the TEM images (Fig. 3 ) it can be clearly seen that the particles are extremely unevenly dispersed on support in 5Ni/SiO2 and 5Ni-SiO2 catalysts, and there exists serious particles agglomeration. For 5Ni/SiO2, it is the small specific area of crystalline SiO2 on which NiO cannot be fully dispersed that result in aggregation. Interestingly, a special aggregation of sheet-like with size of 100–200 nm in 5Ni-SiO2 is found, and the diffraction ring of SEAD (selected area electric diffraction) shows that the special structure is Ni crystal (Fig. S2). Nevertheless, no obvious metal particles can be observed in xNi-SiO2-CQDs catalysts, but the HRTEM image shows the lattice fringe and the lattice spacing is ca. 0.20 nm of the reduced catalyst, which corresponds to the Ni (111) plane. And the particle size observed in the HRTEM images is 3–4 nm (Fig. S3). This indicates that the crystallographic Ni is highly dispersed on the support with the assistance of CQDs, which could explain the disappearance of the characteristic peaks of Ni in Fig. 2. The metal dispersion of catalysts is listed in Table 1, from which it can be seen that the dispersion of active metal of 5Ni/SiO2 and 5Ni-SiO2 is 0.75% and 0.17%, respectively. Obviously, the metal dispersion in xNi-SiO2-CQDs is above 9.4%, much higher than catalysts without CQDs, indicating that the presence of CQDs can greatly promote the metal dispersion. Meanwhile, the surface morphology of reduced catalysts was scanned by SEM (Fig. S4), and it is found that there are many particles on the surface of 5Ni/SiO2, while the surface of xNi-SiO2-CQDs and 5Ni-SiO2 are relatively smooth, illustrating that the metal particles of 5Ni/SiO2 are mainly distributed on the outer surface of support, while in 5Ni-SiO2 and xNi-SiO2-CQDs, the metal is probably dispersed inside the amorphous SiO2. From the EDS mapping and the distribution of Si, O and Ni of 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs catalyst (Fig. S3), it is clear that the distribution of Si, O and Ni is consistent with the sample and thus confirms the uniform distribution of Ni on the support. XPS survey on the reduced catalyst also confirmed the presence of Ni, Si, O elements (Fig. S5). The surface atom ratio of catalysts was calculated by the integrated peak area of the element narrow-spectrum scanning and the result was listed in Table S1. Seen from the result, the Ni content of 5Ni/SiO2 is 16.8%, much higher than the theoretical value (5.11%), which is attribute to the uneven distribution of metal on support and mostly congregated on the surface or the metal particles falls off from the surface of the support. However, the Ni content is merely 0.2% on the surface of 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs, far below the theoretical value (4.40%), which could declare that metal particles in catalyst prepared by in-situ hydrothermal method with CQDs assisted are almost entirely dispersed inside the support, beyond the detect depth of XPS.

The catalytic activity, thermal stability and long-term stability of supported Ni catalysts are strongly influenced by the amount of Ni loading, the size of the dispersed Ni metal particles, metal-support interactions and the chemical state of metal [11,38–43]. The interaction between the metal and support is characterized by H2-TPR (Fig. 4 ). It is evident that there is only one peak at low temperature about 315 °C for 5Ni/SiO2, which can be attribute to the fast reduction of NiO particles on the outside surface of support that have weak interaction with the support. Due to the weak metal-support interaction, the metal particles easily fall off the support and agglomerate into big ones, which would lead to poor thermal stability and long-term stability of catalyst. The 5Ni-SiO2 catalyst shows reduction peaks at 640 °C, which may be due to difficult reduction of the special form NiO with too strong interaction with support. In contrast, the xNi-SiO2-CD catalysts display broad reduction peak at 500 °C, which is due to the slow reduction of NiO inside the pore of support and the strong interaction between the NiO particles and the support [44]. The H2 uptakes and the metal reduction degree at reduction temperature calculated through H2-TPR profiles are listed in Table 1. Seen from the table that the H2 uptakes increase with the increasing metal loading, and the reduction degree of metal in catalyst by in-situ hydrothermal method is much lower than other catalysts, means that proper metal-support interaction could decrease the metal reduction degree. The H2-TPR results show that catalysts synthesized by in-situ hydrothermal method with CQDs assisted strengthen the metal-support interaction to a certain extent, which could inhibit the metal agglomeration during the reduction and reaction.

Deconvolution XPS was conducted to analyze the surface state and chemical surroundings of metal Ni in reduced catalyst. Fig. 5 a presents the high-resolution spectra of Ni 2p in 5Ni/SiO2 and 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs after reduction. The binding energies (BEs) of 852.5 eV and 855.8 eV are attributed to Ni 2p3/2 of Ni0 and Ni2+, and the high BEs at 869.8 eV and 877.8 eV are attributed to Ni 2p1/2 of Ni0 and Ni2+, respectively, whereas the other four peaks are the satellite peaks of Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 [45,46]. The appeared Ni2+ in catalysts is probably due to the surface Ni0 on the support is partially oxidized during the XPS test [47]. Furthermore, it should be mentioned that the BEs of Ni2+ (857.1 eV) and Ni0 (853.4 eV) in 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs are higher than that in 5Ni/SiO2, suggesting that electron transfer between metal and support may occurs in 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs. From the XPS result of O1s (Fig. 5b), we found that there are two O species in Ni/SiO2 catalyst, which attribute to NiO and SiO2, respectively; while there is only one peak in 5Ni/SiO2-CQDs which ascribed to the Ni-O-Si species. The XPS analysis combined with H2-TPR profiles indicates that electron transfer occurs between metal and support, which enhances the interaction between embedded Ni particles and support in 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs.

The comparison of catalytic performance of the Ni-based catalysts by different method can be found in Fig. 6 . The CO conversion of all catalysts below 250 °C is low and may be attributed to the limitation of CO dissociation at low temperature. The CO conversion of catalysts shows a sharp initial increase then plateaus between 200 °C and 500 °C. It can be seen that at lower temperature there are more C2-C4 by-products than CO2, while at higher temperature, the by-products are mostly CO2 and there is nearly no C2-C4. The selectivity of C2-C4 decreases with increasing temperature, while the selectivity of CO2 increases with increasing temperature. The total by-products of catalysts prepared by in-situ hydrothermal method assisted by CQDs are less than those prepared without CQDS. The CH4 selectivity of all catalysts decreases as temperature increases, which may be ascribed to the highly exothermic nature of this reaction [45]. The 5Ni-SiO2 catalyst demonstrates low activity, and the CO conversion is below 10% likely because the special form of Ni has little effect on the dissociation of CO. The CO conversion of 5Ni/SiO2 catalyst increases as the temperature increases, but it is much lower than that of 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs with comparable metal content due to the significant agglomeration of metal particles. xNi-SiO2-CQDs catalysts prepared by in-situ hydrothermal method via CQDs show higher activity compared with those prepared without CQDs or via impregnation. Table S2 shows the comparison of catalytic activity between Ni-supported catalysts for CO methanation when H2/CO = 3/1, and seen from the results that the catalyst prepared by in-situ hydrothermal method with lower metal content exhibits superior catalytic performance. In our experiment, the CO conversion and CH4 selectivity of 1Ni-SiO2-CQDs could reach 95% and 90% at condition of P = 0.1 MPa, WHSV = 15,000mlg-1h−1, H2/CO = 3 and T = 400 °C when the metal loading is as low as 0.90 wt%. Therefore, the catalyst prepared by in-situ hydrothermal method via CQDs can greatly reduce the amount of active metal and save resource.

It is worth noting that when the loading of Ni is 4.40 wt%, the 95% conversion temperature of CO (defined as when CO conversion exceeds 95%) is 300 °C, and that is 350 °C and 400 °C when the amount of Ni is 1.88 wt% and 0.90 wt%, respectively. Thus, a lower metal content requires a higher temperature to completely convert CO, illustrating that the lower the metal content, the lower the specific reaction rate, and the more heat is needed to accelerate the reaction rate. CO dissociation is structural highly sensitive, in this study, the energy barrier in CO dissociation to carbon species via COH intermediate and the hydrogenation of carbon species on Ni (111) facet are calculated by DFT theory (Fig. 7 and Fig. S6). The energy barrier for the formation of intermediate COH is 1.87 eV and that is 1.85 eV for the dissociation of COH to carbon species, which is much higher than the energy barriers for the hydrogenation of carbon species (<0.82 eV). Therefore, at methanation conditions the formation of COH intermediate of H-assisted CO activation is the rate-determining step on Ni-based catalysts. The barriers for COH formation on most hexagonal close-packed (HCP) Ni facets is higher than that on face-centered cubic (FCC) Ni facets [48,49]. Bulk Ni adopts FCC phase under ambient conditions but can transform to HCP phase when particle size was less than 4 nm [50]. Therefore, it is speculated that as the loading of active metal is below 4.40 wt%, the part of active metal particle less than 4 nm is converted from FCC phase to HCP phase, and a higher activation energy is required for the formation of COH intermediate, resulting in a higher temperature to achieve optimal activity. The TOF of the reaction of catalysts with different metal loadings at 250 °C was calculated and listed in the Supporting information (Table S3). It can be seen from the table that with the increase of Ni content, the amount of CO converted on the per active surface Ni of the catalyst at 250 °C increases, indicating that the required activation energy is reduced, which is consistent with the calculated result.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The key role of CQDs in preparation of catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Since in-situ CQDs are generated during the hydrothermal process, UV–Vis spectra, PL spectra and FTIR analysis are used to verify the generation of CQDs and study the role of CQDs in the catalyst preparation process (Fig. 11 ). In the UV/Vis spectra, the peak is 337 nm in aqueous CQDs and Ni-SiO2-CQDs solutions, and Ni-SiO2 solution shows no peak. Meanwhile, the brown solutions under sunlight and the light blue solutions under a dark-box ultraviolet lamp with a 365 nm wavelength indicate the formation of CQDs. Excitation-dependent PL spectra behavior was observed, which is a common phenomenon in fluorescent carbon materials. This behavior can be attributed to the effect of the surface state on the band gaps of CQDs[52,53]. And excitation-dependent PL spectra proves the formation of fluorescent CQDs. In addition, it is obvious that the PL spectra intensity of the Ni-SiO2-CQDs solution is weaker than that of CQDs with the same concentration, indicating that the presence of metal ions weakens the fluorescence effect of CQDs, which is most likely due to the complexation of CQDs and metal ions leading to quenching of CQDs fluorescence [54,55]. In the FTIR analysis of dried CQDs, the following are observed: stretching vibrations and bending vibrations of OH at 3430 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1, stretching vibrations of NH and CH at 3300 cm−1 and 2930 cm−1 [52,56–58]. And the vibrational absorption band of CO at 1700 cm−1, stretching vibration of NH at 1550 cm−1, stretching vibration of amide CN at 1213 cm−1 [59,60] are also observed. However, in the FTIR of CQDs-Ni2+, which is dried after dissolved Ni(NO3)2 in CQDs aqueous solution, the relatively intensity of COOH is changed greatly, and there is an obvious blue shift of δN-H, besides, the peak of amide CN disappears, indicating that the surface groups COOH and NH2 involve in the complexation with Ni2+.

In order to investigate the role of CQDs in catalyst preparation process, 5Ni-SiO2 catalysts were prepared by hydrothermal method without CQDs for comparative study with xNi-SiO2-CQDs. The actual loading of active metal in 5Ni-SiO2, xNi-SiO2-CQDs were listed in Table 1. It is obvious that the loading of Ni in 5Ni-SiO2 (0.88 wt%) is much lower than that in 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs (4.40 wt%) although the amount of metal precursor is equivalent. This is because CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal could complex Ni2+ and then locate in the inner of support; while in the absence of CQDs, the interaction between Ni2+ and support is too weak to retain Ni2+, resulting in the loss of Ni2+ during filtration and washing. The difference of metal loading in 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs and 5Ni-SiO2 indicates that there exists strong interaction between CQDs and metal ions, which could retain on support stably. From the BET results we have known that compared with 5Ni/SiO2 and 5Ni-SiO2, catalysts prepared via CQDs own large surface area above 559 m2g−1 with mesoporous around 10 nm (Fig. 1), thus the synthetic strategy for catalyst design can be described in Fig. 12 . It is known that CA and EDA form CQDs through carbonization passivation during hydrothermal, in which CA serves as carbon source and EDA as surface passivator. And the surface functional groups such as NH2 and COOH complex with metal ions forming stable complex CQDs-metal ions. At the same time, SiO2 formed by TEOS hydrolysis polycondense on the surface of complex, and the stable CQDs-Ni2+ complex is highly dispersed in SiO2 matrix. After calcination to remove CQDs, in-situ NiO nanoparticles are confined in the SiO2 mesoporous, resulting in lower metal loading and strong metal-support interaction, and finally enhancing the catalytic activity and stability.

In-situ CQDs and SiO2 were synthesized by hydrothermal method in this paper, then stable CQDs-Ni2+ complex formed and were highly dispersed in the inner of support, thus catalyst with an ultra-low metal content and metal particles confined into the channel of support was obtained. The results showed that catalyst synthesized by in-situ hydrothermal method exhibited excellent catalytic performance in CO methanation. When the loading of Ni was as low as 0.90 wt%, the CO conversion and CH4 selectivity achieved 95% and 90%, respectively. Meanwhile, the thermal stability and long-term stability of xNi-SiO2-CQDs catalysts were superior to those without CQDs. For 5Ni-SiO2-CQDs, the CO conversion decreased only by 9.7% and CH4 selectivity showed no obvious decrease after catalyst was treated by reactant gas at 650 °C for 2 h. At the same time, in the long-term stability test, the CO conversion and CH4 selectivity decreased slightly at the beginning 20 h, then remained stable at approximately 93% and 90%, respectively. There exists strong interaction between metal and support due to the complexation of CQDs with metal ions, after CQDs are removed by calcination to form mesoporous pores, catalyst possesses a large surface area and the active metal particles are highly dispersed inside the support due to the confinement effect of the pores of support. Finally, CQDs can significantly enhance catalyst stability because that catalysts prepared by in-situ hydrothermal method with CQDs assisted could inhibit the carbon deposition and prevent the agglomeration of metal particles to a degree. The findings presented in this study could be valuable for saving metal resources.

Wenli Gao: Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Qiangfeng Yin: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Xin Meng: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xuelian He: Software. Zhong Xin: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC No. 21808062, 21808064, and 21776091).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fuel.2021.121813.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
